taa.name: WT1
taa.notes: Highest NCI rank. Galinpepimut-S (WT1 vaccine) in Phase III for AML.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: MUC1
taa.expression_normal: apical surface of epithelial cells (polarized, low)
taa.notes: Aberrant glycosylation in tumors exposes neoepitopes. VNTR tandem repeat region is immunodominant.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: LMP2
taa.notes: EBV latent membrane protein. Strong immunogenicity in EBV-seropositive patients.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: HPV E6/E7
taa.fda_approved_therapy: Gardasil/Cervarix (prophylactic); therapeutic vaccines in Phase II/III
taa.notes: Ideal TSA: 100% tumor-specific (viral), required for malignant phenotype. VGX-3100 (DNA vaccine) Phase III.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: EGFRvIII
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (rindopepimut Phase III failed in GBM)
taa.notes: Exon 2-7 deletion creates junction neoepitope PEPvIII (LEEKKGNYVVTDH). 25-30% GBM express EGFRvIII.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: HER-2/neu
taa.fda_approved_therapy: Trastuzumab, pertuzumab (antibodies); NeuVax (E75 peptide vaccine) Phase III
taa.notes: E75 peptide (KIFGSLAFL) is the most clinically advanced cancer peptide vaccine. GP2 and AE37 peptides also in trials.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: EpCAM
taa.notes: Highly expressed on carcinomas. Adecatumumab and solitomab in clinical development.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: MAGE-A3
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (recMAGE-A3+AS15 Phase III MAGRIT failed in NSCLC)
taa.notes: CTA: restricted normal expression (testis is immune-privileged). 30-50% NSCLC, 75% melanoma express MAGE-A3.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: NY-ESO-1

known_epitopes_mhc2.source_note: Zeng et al. J Immunol 2000 — NY-ESO-1 p116-135 HLA-DR4 CD4 epitope

taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (IMCgp100/tebentafusp is for gp100, not NY-ESO-1 directly)
taa.notes: Spontaneous humoral + cellular immunity in >50% patients. Most immunogenic CTA. TCR-T cell therapy (afamitresgene autoleucel) Phase III for synovial sarcoma.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: PSA
taa.fda_approved_therapy: Sipuleucel-T (Provenge, targets PAP not PSA directly); PROSTVAC Phase III completed
taa.notes: PROSTVAC (PSA-TRICOM) Phase III failed but showed trend in intermediate-risk patients.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: PAP
taa.fda_approved_therapy: Sipuleucel-T (Provenge) — FDA approved 2010, first cancer vaccine
taa.notes: Provenge: autologous DC pulsed with PAP-GM-CSF fusion. OS benefit ~4 months in mCRPC.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: CEA
taa.expression_normal: fetal GI tissue; low in adult colon
taa.notes: CAP-1 peptide (YLSGANLNL) extensively tested. Heteroclitic variant CAP1-6D (N→D at P6) showed enhanced immunogenicity.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: gp100
taa.notes: gp100₂₀₉₋₂₁₇ (ITDQVPFSV) + heteroclitic T210M (IMDQVPFSV) is the canonical heteroclitic vaccine example. Tebentafusp is first TCR-based therapy approved.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: MART-1/Melan-A
taa.notes: MART-1₂₆₋₃₅ (EAAGIGILTV) and heteroclitic A27L (ELAGIGILTV) widely used in melanoma vaccine trials. High-frequency T cell precursors in A*02:01+ individuals.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: Tyrosinase
taa.notes: Often combined with gp100 and MART-1 in multi-peptide melanoma vaccines.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: Survivin
taa.expression_normal: fetal tissue; absent in adult differentiated cells
taa.notes: Essential for mitosis → oncogenicity-driven expression. SurVaxM (SVN53-67/M57-KLH) Phase II in GBM.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: PSMA
taa.notes: Major target for prostate cancer. PSMA-targeted CAR-T and bispecifics in Phase I/II.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: GD2
taa.notes: Glycolipid antigen — not directly targetable by peptide vaccines but important for antibody/CAR-T. Included for completeness.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: Mesothelin
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (CRS-207 Listeria vaccine Phase II, anetumab ravtansine)
taa.notes: CRS-207: live attenuated Listeria expressing mesothelin. Tested with GVAX in pancreatic cancer.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: MAGE-A1
taa.notes: First human tumor antigen identified by T cells (Boon et al., 1991).
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: AFP

known_epitopes_mhc1.source_note: Butterfield et al. J Immunol 2001 — AFP542-550 HLA-A*02:01 CTL epitope

taa.notes: AFP-derived peptide vaccines in Phase I/II for HCC.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: MUC16
taa.notes: CA-125 is the shed form used as serum biomarker. Oregovomab (anti-CA-125) as vaccine-like immunotherapy.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: 5T4
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (TroVax/MVA-5T4 Phase III TRIST trial failed in RCC)
taa.notes: MVA-5T4 (TroVax): Modified Vaccinia Ankara expressing 5T4.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: GPC3
taa.expression_normal: fetal liver, placenta (oncofetal, absent in adult liver)
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (GPC3 peptide vaccine Phase II in HCC; codrituzumab Phase II)
taa.notes: Highly HCC-specific. GPC3₂₉₈₋₃₀₆ peptide vaccine showed correlation between CTL response and OS.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: KRAS
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (mRNA-5671/V941 Moderna+Merck Phase I for KRAS-mutant cancers)
taa.notes: G12D, G12V, G12C hotspot mutations. mRNA-5671: personalized KRAS neoantigen mRNA vaccine with pembrolizumab.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: p53
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (p53-SLP vaccine Phase II in ovarian cancer)
taa.notes: Mutant p53 accumulates in tumors. Both WT (overexpressed) and mutant-specific epitopes studied.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: IDH1 R132H
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (NOA-16 IDH1-vac Phase I in glioma — positive results 2021)
taa.notes: IDH1 R132H present in >70% grade II-III gliomas. NOA-16: 20-mer peptide vaccine induced MHC-II response.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: Claudin 18.2
taa.notes: First-in-class anti-CLDN18.2 mAb. mRNA vaccine approaches being explored.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: TERT
taa.fda_approved_therapy: None (GV1001 Phase III in pancreatic cancer; UV1 Phase II)
taa.notes: Near-universal tumor antigen. GV1001: 16-mer hTERT peptide. UV1: 3-peptide hTERT vaccine.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

taa.name: RAS (general)
taa.notes: NRAS Q61 mutations in ~20% melanoma. Often co-targeted with KRAS in RAS vaccine approaches.
taa.nci_rank_citation: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res 2009; PMID:19723653

autoimmune.mechanism: Loss of self-tolerance to CNS myelin antigens. CD4+ Th1/Th17 cells attack myelin sheath.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: Altered peptide ligands (APL); tolerogenic nanoparticles; DNA vaccines
autoimmune.key_trial: NCT01973491 — ATX-MS-1467 (4 MBP peptides) Phase IIa in RRMS
autoimmune.notes: MBP₈₅₋₉₉ is the immunodominant epitope in DR15+ MS patients. APL approach (CGP 77116) showed mixed results — some patients had disease exacerbation.

autoimmune.mechanism: Both humoral (anti-MOG antibodies) and cellular immunity. MOG₃₅₋₅₅ is the dominant CD4 epitope.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: MOG peptide-loaded tolerogenic DCs; inverse vaccines
autoimmune.key_trial: NCT02283671 — tolerogenic DC loaded with MOG/MBP peptides
autoimmune.notes: MOGAD (MOG antibody disease) now recognized as distinct from MS. Therapeutic approach differs.

autoimmune.mechanism: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β cells. GAD65 is a major autoantigen.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: GAD-alum (Diamyd); peptide immunotherapy; inverse nanoparticle vaccines
autoimmune.clinical_status: Phase III (Diamyd GAD-alum in HLA-DR3+ children)
autoimmune.key_trial: NCT03875729 — Diamyd GAD-alum Phase III in recent-onset T1D (positive in DR3+ subgroup)
autoimmune.notes: Diamyd: recombinant GAD65 + alum. Phase III DIAGNODE-3 showed C-peptide preservation in HLA-DR3+ children (2024).

autoimmune.mechanism: Insulin/proinsulin is the primary autoantigen initiating T1D. InsB₉₋₂₃ is the key CD4 epitope.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: Proinsulin DNA vaccine; oral/nasal insulin tolerance; peptide immunotherapy
autoimmune.key_trial: NCT04279613 — PRV-101 (CVB1 vaccine for T1D prevention); BHT-3021 proinsulin DNA vaccine Phase I
autoimmune.notes: InsB₉₋₂₃ presented in register 3 (unusual binding mode) to DQ8/DR4. Tolerogenic approach must avoid boosting pathogenic response.

autoimmune.gene: multiple (vimentin, fibrinogen, α-enolase, type II collagen)
autoimmune.epitope_type: T cell + B cell (ACPA = anti-citrullinated protein antibodies)
autoimmune.mechanism: Citrullination (Arg→citrulline by PAD enzymes) creates neoepitopes. SE+ HLA-DR4 presents citrullinated peptides preferentially.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: Citrullinated peptide tolerogenic DCs; inverse vaccines targeting autoreactive B/T cells
autoimmune.key_trial: Rheumavax (citrullinated peptide-loaded tolerogenic DCs) Phase I — NCT01352884
autoimmune.notes: ACPA+ RA represents 60-70% of RA. Shared epitope (SE) in HLA-DR β chain is the strongest genetic risk factor.

autoimmune.mechanism: Autoimmunity against joint cartilage collagen. CII₂₆₃₋₂₇₀ glycopeptide (galactosylated) is immunodominant.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: Oral collagen tolerance (Colloral); nasal CII peptides
autoimmune.key_trial: Barnett et al., Arthritis Rheum 1998 — Oral CII Phase III showed modest benefit in early RA
autoimmune.notes: Oral tolerance with CII was one of the first antigen-specific immunotherapy approaches in RA.

known_epitopes.source_note: Vader et al. Gastroenterology 2002 — DQ2.5-glia-alpha2 celiac epitope

autoimmune.mechanism: Tissue transglutaminase (tTG) deamidates gliadin peptides → enhanced HLA-DQ2/DQ8 binding → CD4 Th1 response → villous atrophy.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: Nexvax2 (3-peptide desensitization); gluten peptide tolerogenic nanoparticles (TAK-101/TIMP-GLIA)
autoimmune.key_trial: NCT03738475 — CNP-101 (TIMP-GLIA biodegradable nanoparticle) Phase II in celiac disease
autoimmune.notes: Nexvax2 (Phase II) was discontinued in 2019 (did not meet endpoints). TIMP-GLIA nanoparticle approach uses PLG particles loaded with gliadin.

known_epitopes.source_note: Deshmukh et al. J Autoimmun 2011 — SmD79-93 HLA-DR3 lupus epitope

autoimmune.mechanism: Loss of tolerance to nuclear antigens. Immune complexes deposit in kidneys/skin/joints.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: Lupuzor (P140/Rigerimod): phosphopeptide from spliceosomal U1-70K protein
autoimmune.key_trial: NCT02504645 — Lupuzor/Rigerimod Phase III in SLE (completed, mixed results)
autoimmune.notes: Lupuzor (P140): phosphopeptide spanning U1-70K₁₃₁₋₁₅₁. Targets chaperone-mediated autophagy to reduce autoantigen presentation.

autoimmune.epitope_type: B cell (pathogenic IgG) + T cell (CD4 Th2)
autoimmune.mechanism: Anti-DSG3 IgG4 antibodies disrupt desmosomal adhesion → acantholysis (blistering).
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: DSG3-derived tolerogenic peptides; CAR-Treg targeting anti-DSG3 B cells
autoimmune.key_trial: NCT04422223 — DSG3-CAART (anti-DSG3 chimeric autoantibody receptor T cells) Phase I
autoimmune.notes: DSG3-CAART: Tregs expressing chimeric DSG3 to target and suppress anti-DSG3 autoreactive B cells.

autoimmune.mechanism: Anti-AChR antibodies block/destroy neuromuscular junction AChR → muscle weakness.
autoimmune.key_trial: AChR peptide tolerance studies in EAMG (experimental autoimmune MG) models
autoimmune.notes: MG is the prototypical antibody-mediated autoimmune disease. Anti-CD20 (rituximab) is effective, suggesting B cell depletion as alternative.

autoimmune.mechanism: Anti-AQP4 IgG1 activates complement at astrocyte end-feet → CNS inflammation.
autoimmune.vaccine_approach: AQP4 peptide tolerization; non-pathogenic AQP4 IgG decoys
autoimmune.key_trial: Preclinical: AQP4₂₀₁₋₂₂₀ tolerogenic peptide in murine model
autoimmune.notes: Satralizumab (anti-IL6R) and inebilizumab (anti-CD19) are FDA-approved for NMOSD. Tolerogenic approach is emerging.

approved_vaccines.name: BNT162b2 (Comirnaty)
approved_vaccines.notes: Pfizer/BioNTech. Prefusion-stabilized S (K986P+V987P). ~95% efficacy (original strain)

approved_vaccines.name: mRNA-1273 (Spikevax)
approved_vaccines.notes: Moderna. Same 2P stabilization. ~94% efficacy

approved_vaccines.name: ChAdOx1 (Vaxzevria)
approved_vaccines.notes: AstraZeneca/Oxford. Chimpanzee adenovirus vector

approved_vaccines.name: Ad26.COV2.S (Jcovden)
approved_vaccines.notes: J&J. Single dose. Prefusion-stabilized S

approved_vaccines.name: NVX-CoV2373 (Nuvaxovid)
approved_vaccines.notes: Novavax. Recombinant S + saponin adjuvant

infectious.immune_correlate: Neutralizing antibodies (anti-RBD) + S-specific CD4/CD8 T cells
infectious.notes: 2P mutation (K986P+V987P) locks S in prefusion conformation — critical for all approved vaccines. Variant evolution drives updated boosters.

b_cell_epitopes: HA head (antigenic sites Sa, Sb, Ca1, Ca2, Cb)

approved_vaccines.name: QIV (Fluzone, Fluarix)
approved_vaccines.notes: Quadrivalent: 2×A (H1N1+H3N2) + 2×B (Yamagata+Victoria lineage). Egg/cell-based

approved_vaccines.name: FluMist
approved_vaccines.notes: Intranasal. Cold-adapted attenuated virus

approved_vaccines.name: Flublok
approved_vaccines.notes: Sanofi. rHA produced in insect cells (baculovirus). Egg-free

approved_vaccines.name: Fluad
approved_vaccines.notes: Seqirus. For ≥65 years. MF59 squalene adjuvant enhances response

pipeline_vaccines.name: mRNA-1010
pipeline_vaccines.notes: Moderna. Seasonal QIV mRNA vaccine. Phase III

pipeline_vaccines.name: HA stalk-based
pipeline_vaccines.notes: Universal flu vaccine targeting conserved stalk domain

infectious.immune_correlate: HAI titer ≥1:40 (seroprotection); anti-stalk Ab for breadth; CD8 T cells for cross-protection
infectious.notes: Annual strain updating required due to antigenic drift/shift. Universal vaccine targeting HA stalk or M2e is a major goal.

approved_vaccines.name: Arexvy (RSVPreF3-AS01E)
approved_vaccines.notes: GSK. Prefusion F + AS01E. For ≥60 years. ~83% efficacy

approved_vaccines.name: Abrysvo (RSVpreF)
approved_vaccines.notes: Pfizer. RSV A+B prefusion F dimer. For ≥60 years AND maternal immunization. ~82% efficacy

approved_vaccines.name: mResvia (mRNA-1345)
approved_vaccines.notes: Moderna. mRNA encoding prefusion-stabilized F. For ≥60 years. ~84% efficacy

infectious.immune_correlate: Prefusion F-specific neutralizing antibodies (anti-site Ø) + palivizumab-competing Ab
infectious.global_burden: ~33 million LRTI in children <5/year; ~120,000 deaths (primarily developing countries)
infectious.notes: DS-Cav1 structure-based design (McLellan 2013) was breakthrough: stabilized prefusion F enabled all approved RSV vaccines. Maternal vaccination protects neonates.

infectious.disease: Cervical cancer, oropharyngeal cancer, genital warts
b_cell_epitopes: L1 VLP conformational epitopes (type-specific neutralizing Ab)

approved_vaccines.name: Gardasil 9
approved_vaccines.notes: Merck. 9-valent (HPV 6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52,58). ~97% efficacy. Prevents ~90% cervical cancers

approved_vaccines.name: Cervarix
approved_vaccines.notes: GSK. Bivalent (HPV 16,18) + AS04 (alum+MPL). Cross-protection against related types

pipeline_vaccines.name: VGX-3100
pipeline_vaccines.notes: Inovio. DNA encoding HPV16/18 E6+E7. Phase III for CIN2/3

pipeline_vaccines.name: ISA101b
pipeline_vaccines.notes: ISA Pharmaceuticals. HPV16 E6/E7 overlapping long peptides + anti-PD-1

infectious.immune_correlate: Prophylactic: anti-L1 neutralizing Ab; Therapeutic: E6/E7-specific CD8 CTL
infectious.global_burden: ~604,000 new cervical cancer cases/year, 342,000 deaths (WHO 2020). HPV causes ~5% of all cancers
infectious.notes: Most successful cancer prevention vaccine. Therapeutic vaccines target E6/E7 (retained in all HPV+ cancers as they maintain malignant phenotype).

approved_vaccines.name: Engerix-B
approved_vaccines.notes: GSK. Alum-adjuvanted. 3-dose schedule. >95% seroprotection

approved_vaccines.name: Heplisav-B
approved_vaccines.notes: Dynavax. CpG-B TLR9 agonist adjuvant. 2-dose schedule, superior in diabetics

approved_vaccines.name: PreHevbrio
approved_vaccines.notes: VBI Vaccines. Mammalian cell-derived, includes preS1/preS2 domains. 3-dose

pipeline_vaccines.name: Therapeutic HBV vaccines
pipeline_vaccines.notes: Targeting functional cure of chronic HBV. VTP-300 (ChAd/MVA) Phase II

infectious.immune_correlate: Anti-HBs ≥10 mIU/mL (seroprotection); HBV-specific CD8 T cells for therapeutic cure
infectious.global_burden: ~296 million chronic HBV carriers; ~820,000 deaths/year (WHO 2019). Leading cause of HCC
infectious.notes: First recombinant vaccine (1986). Therapeutic vaccines for chronic HBV aim to restore T cell exhaustion — major unmet need.

key_domains: Central NANP repeat region (B cell epitope)

approved_vaccines.name: RTS,S/AS01E (Mosquirix)
approved_vaccines.notes: GSK. CSP NANP repeats + C-term fused to HBsAg VLP. ~36% efficacy over 4 years

approved_vaccines.name: R21/Matrix-M
approved_vaccines.notes: Oxford. Improved CSP-HBsAg ratio vs RTS,S. ~75% efficacy at 1 year

pipeline_vaccines.name: PfSPZ Vaccine
pipeline_vaccines.notes: Sanaria. IV radiation-attenuated sporozoites. ~100% short-term sterile immunity (controlled human malaria)

pipeline_vaccines.name: mRNA malaria vaccines
pipeline_vaccines.notes: BioNTech/Moderna exploring CSP + additional antigens in mRNA format

infectious.immune_correlate: Anti-NANP IgG titer + CSP-specific CD4 T cells + liver-stage CD8 T cells
infectious.global_burden: ~249 million cases, ~608,000 deaths/year (WHO 2022). ~95% in sub-Saharan Africa
infectious.notes: R21/Matrix-M is a game-changer: first malaria vaccine with >75% efficacy. Multi-stage (sporozoite + blood-stage) approaches under development.

b_cell_epitopes: Limited role — TB immunity is primarily T cell mediated

approved_vaccines.name: BCG (Bacille Calmette-Guérin)
approved_vaccines.notes: WHO EPI. Variable efficacy 0-80% in adults. Protects against disseminated TB in children

pipeline_vaccines.name: M72/AS01E
pipeline_vaccines.notes: GSK/Gates MRI. 50% efficacy in Phase IIb (POI trial). Antigens: Mtb32A + Mtb39A. Phase III planned

pipeline_vaccines.name: VPM1002
pipeline_vaccines.notes: Serum Institute. BCG ΔureC::hly (listeriolysin). Phase III in neonates

pipeline_vaccines.name: BNT164
pipeline_vaccines.notes: BioNTech. mRNA-based TB vaccine. Phase I

infectious.immune_correlate: Th1 (IFN-γ producing CD4 T cells) + CD8 CTL; Ab role uncertain
infectious.global_burden: ~10.6 million new cases, ~1.3 million deaths/year (WHO 2022). Leading infectious disease killer
infectious.notes: M72/AS01E is the most advanced new TB vaccine in 100 years. BCG replacement and booster strategies both pursued.

approved_vaccines.name: Ervebo (rVSV-ZEBOV)
approved_vaccines.notes: Merck. VSV backbone with EBOV GP replacing VSV G. Ring vaccination in DRC outbreak. ~97.5% efficacy

approved_vaccines.name: Zabdeno + Mvabea
approved_vaccines.notes: J&J. Ad26.ZEBOV prime + MVA-BN-Filo boost. 2-dose, 56-day interval

infectious.immune_correlate: Anti-GP IgG (binding Ab); GP-specific CD4/CD8 T cells
infectious.global_burden: ~35,000 total cases since 1976; 28,646 in 2013-2016 West Africa outbreak
infectious.notes: Ervebo: first replication-competent viral vector vaccine approved for humans. Ring vaccination strategy was key to outbreak control.

pipeline_vaccines.name: mRNA-1644
pipeline_vaccines.notes: Moderna/IAVI. eOD-GT8 60mer priming for VRC01-class bnAb lineage. Phase I

pipeline_vaccines.name: HVTN 302 (mosaic)
pipeline_vaccines.notes: J&J/HVTN. Mosaic Env antigens. HVTN 706 (Mosaico) Phase III was discontinued 2023

infectious.immune_correlate: Broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) + Env-specific CD4 T cells; no vaccine has achieved durable protection
infectious.global_burden: ~39 million people living with HIV; ~630,000 deaths/year (UNAIDS 2023)
infectious.notes: HIV vaccine remains the greatest unsolved challenge in vaccinology. Germline-targeting sequential immunization (Schief/IAVI approach) is most promising current strategy.

vectors.name: mRNA-LNP (modified mRNA in lipid nanoparticle)
vectors.description: In vitro transcribed mRNA encoding antigen, encapsulated in ionizable lipid nanoparticles (LNP). N1-methylpseudouridine (m1Ψ) modification reduces innate sensing. Cell-free manufacturing.
vectors.capacity_kb: up to ~10 kb mRNA (practical: 1-4 kb for single antigen)
vectors.safety_profile: reactogenicity (fever, myalgia common); rare myocarditis (young males); no genome integration risk
advantages: Fastest development timeline (weeks from sequence to candidate)
No anti-vector immunity — unlimited re-dosing
Strong CD8 T cell induction (cytoplasmic expression)
Cell-free manufacturing — no cell culture contamination risk
Sequence easily updated for variants
Self-adjuvanting (LNP + dsRNA byproducts activate innate immunity)
limitations: Cold chain requirement (-20°C or colder)
Short mRNA half-life in vivo (~24-72h expression)
LNP reactogenicity (PEG-lipid allergies in rare cases)
Anti-PEG antibodies may reduce efficacy of repeat dosing
Limited insert size (~10 kb max)
Higher cost than traditional vaccines

design_parameters.5'UTR: Optimized for translation (α-globin or custom UTR); avoid uAUGs
design_parameters.CDS: Codon-optimized for human; deplete uridine; minimize CpG
design_parameters.polyA: 100-150 nt segmented poly(A) tail (A30LA70 for BNT)
design_parameters.LNP_composition: Ionizable lipid (SM-102/ALC-0315) : DSPC : cholesterol : PEG-lipid = 50:10:38.5:1.5 mol%

vectors.notes: Dominant platform post-COVID. BioNTech and Moderna are leaders. Key innovation: m1Ψ modification (Karikó & Weissman, Nobel 2023) + LNP delivery.

vectors.name: Self-amplifying RNA (saRNA / replicon)
vectors.description: mRNA with alphavirus replicon (nsP1-4 replicase) enabling self-amplification in host cells. Lower dose needed but higher innate immune activation.
approved_products: ARCT-154 (ARCT saRNA, approved in Japan 2023 for COVID-19)
vectors.cold_chain: 2-8°C (more stable than conventional mRNA due to lower dose)
vectors.duration_of_immunity: potentially longer than conventional mRNA (sustained expression)
vectors.capacity_kb: ~2-4 kb antigen insert (replicon adds ~7 kb backbone)
vectors.safety_profile: higher reactogenicity than conventional mRNA (dsRNA replication intermediates)
vectors.scalability: very high (10-100x lower dose → more doses per batch)
advantages: 10-100x lower dose needed vs conventional mRNA
Prolonged antigen expression (days to weeks)
Stronger innate immune activation (self-adjuvanting)
Lower manufacturing cost per dose
limitations: Larger construct (~12 kb total) — harder to encapsulate
Higher reactogenicity
Less clinical experience than conventional mRNA
Cannot use m1Ψ modification (replicase requires unmodified U)

design_parameters.backbone: Venezuelan equine encephalitis (VEE) or Sindbis replicon (nsP1-4)
design_parameters.LNP: Similar to conventional mRNA-LNP but larger particle size (~100-150 nm)

vectors.notes: ARCT-154 (Arcturus/CSL) is the first approved saRNA vaccine. Gritstone and Imperial College also developing saRNA platforms.

vectors.name: DNA vaccine (plasmid + electroporation)
vectors.description: Plasmid DNA encoding antigen under CMV/CAG promoter, delivered via intramuscular injection + electroporation to enhance uptake.
vectors.immune_response: both (but generally weaker than mRNA without EP)
vectors.manufacturing: bacterial fermentation (E. coli) — very low cost
vectors.duration_of_immunity: months (potentially durable due to sustained expression)
vectors.safety_profile: excellent safety record; electroporation causes local pain; theoretical integration risk (extremely low in practice)
advantages: Room temperature stable — no cold chain needed
Cheapest manufacturing cost
No size limit for insert
Excellent safety record (>20 years of clinical data)
Easy to design multi-antigen constructs
limitations: Lower immunogenicity than mRNA (needs electroporation)
Electroporation device adds complexity and cost
Slow nuclear import reduces efficiency
Few approved products (limited regulatory track record)

design_parameters.promoter: CMV immediate-early or CAG hybrid promoter
design_parameters.backbone: Minimize CpG in backbone (minicircle DNA) or use CpG-free vector
design_parameters.delivery: CELLECTRA® EP device (Inovio) or PharmaJet Tropis® (needle-free)

vectors.notes: VGX-3100 (HPV E6/E7 DNA + EP) is most advanced therapeutic DNA vaccine. Inovio INO-4800 (COVID) showed T cell responses but struggled with efficacy endpoints.

vectors.name: Adenovirus vector (human Ad5, Ad26; chimpanzee ChAdOx1)
vectors.description: Replication-deficient adenovirus (E1/E3 deleted) expressing antigen from transgene cassette. Strong innate immune activation via DNA sensing pathways.
vectors.cd8_induction: strong (DNA enters nucleus → robust MHC-I cross-presentation)
vectors.dose_schedule: 1-2 doses (heterologous boost recommended for Ad vectors)
vectors.onset_of_immunity: 14 days (single dose sufficient for many Ad26 vaccines)
vectors.duration_of_immunity: months to years (stronger durability than mRNA for T cells)
vectors.pre_existing_immunity: HIGH for Ad5 (40-90% global seroprevalence); LOW for Ad26 (~25%); VERY LOW for ChAdOx1 (chimpanzee origin)
vectors.safety_profile: generally well-tolerated; rare TTS (thrombosis with thrombocytopenia) with ChAdOx1/Ad26; anti-PF4 autoantibodies
advantages: Strong and durable CD8 T cell responses
Single dose may be sufficient (Ad26)
Stable at 2-8°C (lyophilized: room temp)
Proven in Ebola, COVID, HIV trials — extensive clinical data
ChAdOx1/Ad26 avoid human Ad5 pre-existing immunity issue
limitations: Anti-vector immunity limits re-dosing with same vector
Ad5 high seroprevalence globally (especially Africa, Asia)
TTS safety signal (rare but serious)
Cell-based manufacturing slower than mRNA
~7 kb insert size limit (standard E1/E3 deleted)

design_parameters.vector_choice: Ad26 (low seroprevalence) or ChAdOx1 (chimpanzee, no human seroprevalence)
design_parameters.transgene: Codon-optimized antigen; prefusion stabilization for viral glycoproteins
design_parameters.E1_deletion: Mandatory (replication-deficient); supplied in trans by production cell line

vectors.notes: Heterologous prime-boost (e.g., ChAd prime → mRNA boost) may be optimal strategy. Anti-vector immunity is the main limitation for homologous boosting.

vectors.name: Modified Vaccinia Ankara (MVA)
vectors.description: Highly attenuated poxvirus vector (>6 passages in chicken embryo fibroblasts, lost 15% of genome). Cannot replicate in human cells. Large insert capacity.
vectors.cd8_induction: strong (large genome activates multiple innate pathways)
vectors.manufacturing: cell-based (chicken embryo fibroblasts, DF-1 cells, AGE1.CR.pIX)
vectors.dose_schedule: typically boost in heterologous prime-boost regimens
vectors.pre_existing_immunity: low in post-smallpox vaccination era (<40 years old); moderate in older adults
vectors.safety_profile: excellent (cannot replicate in immunocompromised); safe in HIV+ individuals
advantages: Very large insert capacity (~25 kb) — multi-antigen constructs
Cannot replicate in human cells — safe in immunocompromised
Strong innate immune activation
Low pre-existing immunity in younger populations
Proven heterologous boost partner for Ad26 (Ebola, HIV regimens)
limitations: Manufacturing complexity (cell-based, lower yields than Ad vectors)
Pre-existing immunity in older adults (smallpox vaccination)
Single-use vector (anti-vector immunity after first exposure)

design_parameters.insertion_sites: del III, TK locus, or IGR (intergenic regions)
design_parameters.promoter: p7.5 (early/late) or mH5 (strong early/late) poxvirus promoter
design_parameters.multi_antigen: Multiple expression cassettes possible due to large capacity

vectors.notes: MVA is the standard heterologous boost partner. Ad26-prime + MVA-boost is the backbone of J&J's Ebola and HIV vaccine programs.

vectors.name: VSV (Vesicular Stomatitis Virus) vector
vectors.description: Replication-competent recombinant VSV where VSV G protein is replaced with target pathogen glycoprotein. Single-dose, strong mucosal immunity.
vectors.safety_profile: arthritis, rash (transient viral replication); contraindicated in severely immunocompromised
advantages: Single dose — rapid immunity onset
Replication-competent — strong immune response
No pre-existing immunity in humans
Proven in outbreak response (Ebola ring vaccination)
limitations: Replication-competent — not suitable for immunocompromised
Cold chain (-60°C or colder)
Limited insert size
Transient viral symptoms (arthritis, vesicular lesions)

design_parameters.backbone: Indiana serotype VSV with G gene deleted
design_parameters.glycoprotein: Target pathogen GP replaces VSV G (provides tropism + immunity)

vectors.notes: Proof of concept for replication-competent vector vaccines. Investigated for SARS-CoV-2, Marburg, Lassa.

vectors.name: Recombinant protein subunit + adjuvant
vectors.description: Purified recombinant protein antigen (produced in CHO, insect, yeast, or plant cells) formulated with adjuvant to enhance immunogenicity.
vectors.cd8_induction: weak (exogenous protein → MHC-II primarily; cross-presentation limited)
advantages: Well-understood regulatory pathway (decades of experience)
Stable at 2-8°C
No genetic material — no integration risk
Flexible adjuvant selection to tune immune response
Can be produced in multiple expression systems
limitations: Weak CD8 T cell induction without specialized adjuvants
Requires adjuvant (adds cost, complexity, and reactogenicity)
Slower development (protein expression + purification optimization)
Conformation-dependent epitopes may be lost during purification

design_parameters.expression_system: CHO (mammalian, glycosylation); Sf9/High Five (baculovirus); Pichia/S. cerevisiae (yeast); N. benthamiana (plant)
design_parameters.antigen_design: Prefusion stabilization (2P mutation for coronavirus/RSV); domain-focused (RBD vs full-length)
design_parameters.formulation: Nanoparticle display (SpyCatcher, ferritin, mi3) enhances B cell response

vectors.notes: Nanoparticle display of antigens (e.g., ferritin-RBD, SpyCatcher-VLP) is a major innovation, enhancing B cell activation through multivalent engagement.

vectors.name: Virus-Like Particle (VLP)
vectors.description: Self-assembling protein nanoparticles that mimic virus structure but contain no genetic material. Repetitive surface display of antigens triggers strong B cell responses.
vectors.immune_response: strong humoral (B cell activation via BCR crosslinking)
vectors.cd8_induction: weak to moderate (cross-presentation depends on size and composition)
vectors.duration_of_immunity: years to decades (HPV: >14 years; HBV: >30 years)
vectors.capacity_kb: N/A (protein assembly, surface display of antigens)
vectors.pre_existing_immunity: none (unless carrier VLP has been used before, e.g., HBsAg)
advantages: Highly immunogenic (multivalent, repetitive epitope display)
Proven long-term durability (HPV: single vaccination may suffice for lifetime)
No genetic material — inherently safe
Can display foreign antigens (e.g., RTS,S: malaria CSP on HBsAg VLP)
Elicit strong germinal center reactions
limitations: Limited to surface-displayed antigens (conformational B cell epitopes)
Self-assembly requirements constrain antigen design
Weak CD8 T cell induction for intracellular antigens
Manufacturing optimization for correct VLP assembly can be challenging

design_parameters.scaffold: HBsAg (22nm), HPV L1 (55nm), bacteriophage AP205/Qβ (28nm), ferritin (12nm), mi3 (28nm)
design_parameters.display: Genetic fusion, SpyCatcher/SpyTag conjugation, or chemical coupling
design_parameters.valency: 60-360 copies per particle (optimal for B cell crosslinking)

vectors.notes: VLP technology dates to 1981 (HBV). SpyCatcher/SpyTag plug-and-display VLP system enables rapid antigen swapping.

vectors.name: Live Attenuated Vaccine (LAV)
vectors.description: Weakened but replication-competent pathogen. Mimics natural infection, inducing broad humoral + cellular + mucosal immunity.
vectors.safety_profile: contraindicated in immunocompromised; rare reversion to virulence (OPV); excellent safety in immunocompetent
advantages: Broadest immune response (humoral + cellular + mucosal + innate memory)
Often single dose → lifelong immunity
Cheapest to manufacture
Natural antigen presentation (all proteins, correct conformation)
Induces trained immunity (BCG non-specific effects)
limitations: Contraindicated in immunocompromised/pregnant
Risk of reversion to virulence (OPV → cVDPV)
Cannot be used for all pathogens (BSL-4, slow-growing, oncogenic)
Cold chain sensitive
Long development time (serial passage for attenuation)

design_parameters.attenuation: Serial passage in non-human cells (classical); codon-pair deoptimization (modern); deletion of virulence genes (rational design)
design_parameters.genetic_stability: Monitor for reversion mutations at each passage

vectors.notes: YF-17D is the gold standard LAV — single dose, lifelong immunity, manufactured since 1937. Modern rational attenuation (codon-pair deoptimization) enables designer LAVs.

vectors.name: Inactivated whole-virus vaccine
vectors.description: Chemically or physically inactivated pathogen retaining surface antigen structure. Safe but weaker cellular immunity than LAV.
advantages: Simple, well-understood technology
Safe in immunocompromised
Broad antigen repertoire (all viral proteins preserved)
Room temperature stability possible
limitations: Weaker cellular immunity
Requires adjuvant
Multiple doses needed
Inactivation may damage conformational epitopes
Requires BSL-3/4 for pathogen growth (high-containment for dangerous pathogens)

design_parameters.inactivation: β-propiolactone (preferred for envelope viruses) or formaldehyde (classical)
design_parameters.adjuvant: Alum (most common) or novel adjuvants for enhanced response
design_parameters.QC: Verify complete inactivation; confirm antigen integrity post-inactivation

vectors.notes: Backbone of traditional vaccinology. CoronaVac/BBIBP-CorV were critical for global COVID vaccination (>5 billion doses in China, Asia, South America).

adjuvants.name: Aluminum salts (Alum)
adjuvants.mechanism: Depot effect (slow antigen release); NLRP3 inflammasome activation; enhances antigen uptake by APCs; promotes Th2 immune response.
adjuvants.safety_profile: excellent (>90 years of use); injection site reactions (pain, redness); rare granuloma; no systemic safety concerns at standard doses
adjuvants.formulation_notes: Antigen adsorbed onto aluminum salt particles (1-10 μm). Al(OH)₃ for positively charged antigens; AlPO₄ for negatively charged. Must maintain pH and prevent aggregation.
advantages: Longest safety track record of any adjuvant (since 1926)
Low cost and widely available
Compatible with most protein antigens
Regulatory acceptance worldwide
Stable at 2-8°C
limitations: Th2-biased — poor CD8 T cell and Th1 induction
Inadequate for intracellular pathogens requiring cellular immunity
Does not work well with polysaccharide antigens
Cannot be frozen (irreversible aggregation)
Insufficient for elderly/immunocompromised (weak immune potentiation)
adjuvants.notes: Default comparator adjuvant in clinical trials. Used in >80% of adjuvanted vaccines globally. For cancer/TB/HIV vaccines requiring Th1/CD8, alum alone is insufficient.

adjuvants.name: MF59
adjuvants.mechanism: Oil-in-water squalene nanoemulsion (160 nm). Creates 'immunocompetent environment' at injection site: recruits neutrophils → monocytes → DCs. Enhances antigen uptake, transport to dLN, and GC reactions.
innate_receptors: MyD88-independent; ASC/NLRP3 partial; ATP/muscle damage sensing
adjuvants.immune_profile: balanced (Th1 + Th2; stronger than alum for both)
adjuvants.safety_profile: well-tolerated; injection site pain/swelling more than alum; no long-term safety signals in >200 million doses
adjuvants.formulation_notes: Squalene 9.75 mg + Tween 80 1.175 mg + Span 85 1.175 mg per 0.25 mL. Microfluidized to 160 nm droplets. Mixed with antigen at point-of-use.
advantages: Stronger and broader immunity than alum
Dose-sparing effect (critical for pandemic stockpiling)
Good safety record (~30 years, >200 million doses)
Enhances responses in elderly (approved for ≥65 years)
Works with influenza split-virion and subunit antigens
limitations: Proprietary (Seqirus/Novartis) — availability constraints
Not as strong for Th1/CD8 as AS01 or saponin adjuvants
Squalene sourced from shark liver (ethical/supply concerns) — synthetic alternatives emerging
adjuvants.notes: Seqirus (Novartis successor) holds exclusive rights. WHO pandemic stockpile includes MF59-adjuvanted H5N1 vaccine. Synthetic squalene now available.

adjuvants.name: AS03
adjuvants.mechanism: Oil-in-water emulsion (squalene + DL-α-tocopherol + Tween 80). α-tocopherol (vitamin E) provides unique immunostimulatory activity: enhances NF-κB signaling in monocytes.
innate_receptors: similar to MF59 + α-tocopherol-specific monocyte activation
adjuvants.immune_profile: balanced (Th1 + Th2; very strong GC reactions)
adjuvants.safety_profile: injection site reactions; fatigue; rare association with narcolepsy (Pandemrix in Scandinavia — likely antigen-specific, not adjuvant per se)
adjuvants.formulation_notes: Squalene 10.68 mg + DL-α-tocopherol 11.86 mg + Tween 80 4.85 mg per 0.25 mL. Two-vial system (antigen + adjuvant mixed at site).
advantages: Exceptional dose-sparing (up to 4-6x for pandemic flu)
Strong germinal center and memory B cell induction
Cross-reactive antibody broadening (important for influenza variants)
limitations: Narcolepsy signal with Pandemrix (resolved as antigen-related)
Proprietary (GSK)
Higher reactogenicity than alum
adjuvants.notes: Key pandemic preparedness adjuvant. AS03 enabled dose-sparing that stretched H1N1 vaccine supply 4-6 fold during 2009 pandemic.

adjuvants.name: Matrix-M
adjuvants.mechanism: Saponin (Fraction-A + Fraction-C from Quillaja saponaria) formulated into 40 nm nanoparticles with cholesterol + phospholipid. Activates innate immunity, enhances antigen trafficking to dLN, promotes strong GC reactions.
innate_receptors: NLRP3 inflammasome; caspase-1; IL-18 release; cholesterol-dependent membrane disruption
adjuvants.safety_profile: injection site pain, fatigue, headache; generally well-tolerated; no serious safety signals
adjuvants.formulation_notes: Novavax proprietary. ISCOM-like matrix (40 nm cage-like nanoparticles). Can be co-formulated with protein antigens.
advantages: Strong Th1 induction (better than alum for cellular immunity)
Enhances antibody quality (affinity maturation, GC reactions)
Nanoparticle format — no separate adjuvant vial needed
Proven in malaria vaccine (R21: ~75% efficacy attributable in part to Matrix-M)
Sustainable Quillaja saponin sourcing being developed (plant cell culture)
limitations: Proprietary (Novavax) — limited availability for third parties
Quillaja tree sustainability concerns (wild harvest)
Higher reactogenicity than alum
adjuvants.notes: Matrix-M's role in R21 malaria vaccine success is notable — same CSP antigen as RTS,S but Matrix-M >> AS01E for this formulation.

adjuvants.name: AS01B / AS01E
adjuvants.mechanism: Liposome containing MPL (3-O-desacyl-4'-monophosphoryl lipid A, TLR4 agonist) + QS-21 (saponin, from Quillaja saponaria). Dual innate activation: TLR4 (MPL) + NLRP3/caspase-1 (QS-21). Liposome delivery targets APCs.
adjuvants.safety_profile: higher reactogenicity than alum (injection site pain, fever, fatigue are common); Grade 3 reactions in ~10%; no serious safety concerns (>100 million Shingrix doses)
adjuvants.regulatory_status: approved (AS01B in Shingrix, AS01E in Arexvy/Mosquirix)
adjuvants.dose_range: AS01B: MPL 50 μg + QS-21 50 μg; AS01E: MPL 25 μg + QS-21 25 μg (half-dose)
adjuvants.formulation_notes: MPL and QS-21 co-formulated in DOPC/cholesterol liposomes. Two-vial system: lyophilized antigen + liquid AS01. Reconstitute at point-of-use.
advantages: Strongest clinically approved adjuvant for T cell immunity
Shingrix: 97% efficacy in ≥50 years, >90% in immunocompromised — benchmark
Dual mechanism (TLR4 + saponin) provides robust Th1 polarization
Effective in elderly and immunocompromised (critical populations)
Cross-presentation enhancement for CD8 T cells
limitations: High reactogenicity (limits acceptability in healthy young adults)
Complex formulation (liposome + two active components)
Proprietary (GSK) — limited third-party access
QS-21 supply constraints (Quillaja tree)
High cost (~$150-200 per Shingrix course)
adjuvants.notes: AS01B/E is the gold standard for vaccines requiring strong cellular immunity. M72/AS01E's 50% efficacy against TB is the first new TB vaccine signal in 100 years.

adjuvants.name: CpG-1018 (CpG-B ODN)
adjuvants.mechanism: Synthetic CpG-B class oligodeoxynucleotide (22-mer). Activates TLR9 in plasmacytoid DCs and B cells → type I IFN production, B cell proliferation, Th1 polarization.
adjuvants.safety_profile: well-tolerated; injection site reactions; mild systemic symptoms; no autoimmune signals (initial concern resolved after Phase III + post-marketing surveillance)
adjuvants.formulation_notes: Phosphorothioate backbone (nuclease-resistant). Mixed with antigen in saline. No lipid carrier needed.
advantages: Synthetic, fully defined molecule — batch consistency
2-dose schedule for HBV (vs 3-dose with alum) — better compliance
Superior seroprotection in diabetics and elderly (vs alum-HBV)
Strong Th1 polarization without emulsion/liposome complexity
Scalable synthetic manufacturing
limitations: Single approved product (limited clinical experience breadth)
Requires high dose (3 mg) — moderate cost
Th1-focused — may need combination for balanced response
TLR9 expression varies across species (mouse >> human in pDC)
adjuvants.notes: Dynavax product. CpG-1018 enabled 2-dose HBV schedule and improved responses in hard-to-immunize populations. CpG-B class (vs CpG-A) selected for B cell activation.

adjuvants.name: AS04 (MPL + Alum)
adjuvants.mechanism: 3-O-desacyl-monophosphoryl lipid A (MPL, TLR4 agonist) adsorbed onto aluminum hydroxide. MPL provides Th1 signal; alum provides depot + Th2 component → balanced response.
innate_receptors: TLR4 (cell surface) via TRIF-dependent pathway (biased signaling)
adjuvants.safety_profile: slightly higher reactogenicity than alum alone; excellent long-term safety (>100 million Cervarix doses)
adjuvants.dose_range: MPL 50 μg + Al(OH)₃ 500 μg per dose
adjuvants.formulation_notes: MPL adsorbed onto Al(OH)₃. Single-vial formulation (pre-mixed). MPL derived from Salmonella minnesota R595 LPS.
advantages: Proven cross-protective immunity (Cervarix cross-protects against HPV31/33/45)
Simple formulation (pre-mixed, single vial)
Enhances GC reactions and memory B cells vs alum alone
Long-duration antibody response
limitations: Less potent than AS01 for cellular immunity
Proprietary (GSK)
MPL production from bacterial LPS is complex
adjuvants.notes: AS04 in Cervarix produces broader cross-protection against non-vaccine HPV types than Gardasil (alum only), attributed to stronger GC reactions and antibody affinity maturation.

adjuvants.name: STING agonists (cGAMP, ADU-S100, MSA-2)
adjuvants.mechanism: Activate STING (Stimulator of IFN Genes) → TBK1 → IRF3 → type I IFN production. Powerful induction of innate immunity, DC maturation, and cross-presentation.
adjuvants.safety_profile: Phase I/II — local inflammation, flu-like symptoms; dose-limiting toxicity at high doses (intratumoral)
adjuvants.regulatory_status: Phase I/II (vaccine adjuvant); Phase II (cancer immunotherapy)
adjuvants.formulation_notes: cGAMP: endogenous ligand. Synthetic CDNs: ADU-S100 (Aduro), MK-1454 (Merck). Non-nucleotide: MSA-2 (oral bioavailable).
advantages: Potent CD8 T cell induction (strongest among emerging adjuvants)
Bridges innate and adaptive immunity via type I IFN
Effective for cancer vaccines and therapeutic applications
Oral bioavailable versions in development (MSA-2)
limitations: Not yet approved as vaccine adjuvant
Narrow therapeutic window (too much → immunopathology)
Rapid degradation of CDN in vivo — requires formulation (LNP, hydrogel)
STING polymorphisms in human populations (R232H) may affect response
adjuvants.notes: STING agonists are the most promising adjuvant class for cancer vaccines requiring CD8 T cell responses. BioNTech exploring STING agonist + mRNA combinations.

adjuvants.name: Poly-ICLC (Hiltonol)
aliases: polyinosinic-polycytidylic acid stabilized with poly-L-lysine and carboxymethylcellulose
adjuvants.mechanism: Synthetic dsRNA analog. Activates TLR3 (endosomal) + MDA5/RIG-I (cytoplasmic) → type I IFN, DC maturation, strong Th1/CD8 T cell responses.
adjuvants.safety_profile: flu-like symptoms (fever, fatigue, myalgia) — mimics viral infection; generally well-tolerated at 1-2 mg doses
adjuvants.formulation_notes: Oncovir, Inc. Stabilized poly(I:C) formulation prevents degradation by RNases. Can be mixed with peptide/protein antigens.
advantages: Potent type I IFN inducer — 'viral mimic' adjuvant
Strong CD8 T cell responses (ideal for cancer/HIV vaccines)
Extensive Phase I/II data in cancer vaccine trials
Non-proprietary formulation concept
limitations: Not yet approved for any vaccine
Flu-like symptoms limit acceptability for prophylactic vaccines
Batch-to-batch variability (polymer)
adjuvants.notes: Widely used in cancer neoantigen vaccine trials (e.g., NeoVax by Catherine Wu/Dana-Farber: personal neoantigen peptides + poly-ICLC → durable CD4/CD8 responses in melanoma, PMID: 28678778).

adjuvants.name: LNP (Lipid Nanoparticle) as self-adjuvant
adjuvants.mechanism: Ionizable lipid nanoparticles used for mRNA delivery ALSO function as adjuvants: (1) ionizable lipid activates intracellular innate sensors; (2) LNP formulation recruits neutrophils/monocytes to injection site; (3) IL-6 production independent of mRNA cargo.
adjuvants.immune_profile: balanced Th1/Th2 (mRNA content provides additional Th1 via dsRNA sensing)
adjuvants.cd8_enhancement: moderate (from LNP alone); strong (with mRNA cargo — cytoplasmic translation → MHC-I)
adjuvants.safety_profile: injection site reactions, fever, fatigue (attributed partly to LNP adjuvant effect, not just mRNA)
adjuvants.regulatory_status: approved (as delivery vehicle with intrinsic adjuvant properties)
adjuvants.formulation_notes: SM-102 (Moderna) or ALC-0315 (BioNTech): ionizable at pH<6.5 → endosomal escape. Standard 4-component LNP: ionizable lipid + DSPC + cholesterol + PEG-lipid.
advantages: No separate adjuvant needed for mRNA vaccines
Dual function: delivery + immune activation
Well-characterized and approved formulation
limitations: Reactogenicity (attributed to LNP innate activation)
Anti-PEG antibodies after repeated dosing
LNP distribution to liver (off-target expression)
adjuvants.notes: Recognition that LNP itself has adjuvant activity (Alameh et al., Immunity 2022) changed how mRNA vaccines are understood — it's not just mRNA, the vehicle contributes significantly to immunogenicity.

tcr_clones.clinical_use: Parental TCR for afamitresgene autoleucel (Adaptimmune) — Phase III synovial sarcoma (SPEARHEAD-1, NCT04044768)
tcr_clones.notes: Verify: RCSB → 2BNR → Sequences tab → chain D (α) and chain E (β). CDR3 boundaries per IMGT.

tcr_clones.disease_context: synovial sarcoma, myxoid round cell liposarcoma
tcr_clones.clinical_use: Affinity-enhanced backbone of afamitresgene autoleucel (Adaptimmune) — Phase III (SPEARHEAD-1)
tcr_clones.notes: Verify: RCSB → 2P5E → Sequences → chain E shows engineered CDR3β. α chain identical to WT 1G4.

tcr_clones.clinical_use: FDA-APPROVED: Tebentafusp (KIMMTRAK) — Jan 2022 uveal melanoma. First TCR-based therapy approved. Phase III IMCgp100-202 (NCT03070392).
tcr_clones.notes: Primary citation: NEJM phase 3 IMCgp100-202 (tebentafusp vs investigator choice) in HLA-A*02:01+ metastatic uveal melanoma; bispecific TCR–anti-CD3 targeting gp100-presenting cells. Former PDB 6RPB + J Immunol 2020 pair describe NY-ESO-1 TCR NYE_S1 + SLLMWITQV, not gp100/YLEPGPVTA. Public gp100 peptide in HLA-A2 without TCR: PDB 1TVB (ITDQVPFSV, residues 209–217), a different register than YLEPGPVTA.

tcr_clones.clinical_use: NCI Phase I/II TCR-T — 30% objective response in metastatic melanoma (Morgan et al., Science 2006). First successful TCR gene transfer trial.
tcr_clones.notes: Verify: RCSB → 3QDG → Sequences tab. Heteroclitic MART-1₂₆₋₃₅ A27L decamer. On-target/off-tumor toxicity in skin, eyes, ears.

tcr_clones.clinical_use: CLINICAL SAFETY EVENT: Affinity-enhanced version (Adaptimmune) caused 2 fatal cardiac toxicities — cross-reactivity with Titin (ESDPIVAQY). Trial halted.
tcr_clones.notes: Verify: RCSB → 5BRZ → Sequences tab. CRITICAL SAFETY: demonstrates danger of TCR affinity engineering beyond self-tolerance threshold.

tcr_motifs.epitope_note: Native sequence (Cys at pos 165); SLLMWITQV is a synthetic analog used in research
tcr_motifs.cdr3b_motif: Three sets: AV3/BV8S2, AV1/BV1, AV3/BV13 — all recognize via central Met/Trp contacts
tcr_motifs.cdr3a_motif: Conserved CDR3 length and motif within each set (see Garin 2008 Fig. 2)
tcr_motifs.frequency_in_population: Detectable in most NY-ESO-1-seropositive cancer patients
tcr_motifs.notes: Garin et al. PNAS 2008. Three convergent αβ TCR sets recognize SLLMWITQC via conserved CDR3 motifs. Foundation for afamitresgene autoleucel (Phase III). Old nomenclature: BV8S2=TRBV12-3/4, BV1=TRBV9, BV13=TRBV6-5 in IMGT.

tcr_motifs.epitope_note: A27L heteroclitic analog of native MART-1₂₇₋₃₅ (AAGIGILTV); enhanced immunogenicity
tcr_motifs.cdr3b_motif: Public GxG motif at positions 110-112-113 of CDR3β (Speiser 2008 / Iancu 2009)
tcr_motifs.cdr3a_motif: TRAV12-2 with conserved CDR1α contacts to peptide
tcr_motifs.frequency_in_population: ~1/10,000 naive T cells in HLA-A*02:01+ healthy donors
tcr_motifs.notes: Iancu et al. J Transl Med 2009. Identified public CDR3 motif (GxG) and TRBV28 bias across melanoma patients. TRAV12-2 CDR1α makes critical contacts with peptide. DMF5 TCR (NCI trials) uses TRAV12-2/TRBV6-4.

data_sources.nci_rankings: Cheever MA et al. Clin Cancer Res. 2009;15(17):5323-37. PMID:19723653
data_sources.epitopes: IEDB (Immune Epitope Database) — www.iedb.org. Vita R et al. Nucleic Acids Res. 2019;47(D1):D339-D343.

_meta.integrity_note: Epitope links verified against IEDB IQ-API. NCI rankings from PMID:19723653. PDB structures verified via RCSB REST API. No AI-generated citations.

verification_pass2.method: IEDB IQ-API batch verification + manual sequence correction
